explanation.title: Working with Text: Java Strings and Their Methods
explanation.content:
# Strings in Java

In Java, `String` is not a primitive type — it is a **class**. This means String values are objects with built-in methods you call using dot notation.

```java
String name = "Priya Sharma";
System.out.println(name.length());        // 12
System.out.println(name.toUpperCase());   // PRIYA SHARMA
```

## String Concatenation

The `+` operator joins strings:

```java
String first = "Rahul";
String last  = "Sharma";
String full  = first + " " + last;  // "Rahul Sharma"
```

When `+` is used with a non-String (like an `int`), Java converts it to String automatically:

```java
int score = 95;
System.out.println("Score: " + score);  // Score: 95
```

## Essential String Methods

| Method | Returns | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `.length()` | int — character count | `"Hello".length()` → `5` |
| `.toUpperCase()` | String | `"hello".toUpperCase()` → `"HELLO"` |
| `.toLowerCase()` | String | `"HELLO".toLowerCase()` → `"hello"` |
| `.trim()` | String — no surrounding spaces | `" hi ".trim()` → `"hi"` |
| `.charAt(i)` | char at index `i` | `"Hello".charAt(0)` → `'H'` |
| `.substring(s, e)` | String from index `s` to `e` | `"Hello".substring(1, 4)` → `"ell"` |
| `.contains(s)` | boolean | `"Hello".contains("ell")` → `true` |
| `.replace(a, b)` | String | `"cats".replace("cats", "dogs")` → `"dogs"` |
| `.equals(s)` | boolean — exact match | `"hi".equals("hi")` → `true` |
| `.equalsIgnoreCase(s)` | boolean — case-insensitive | `"Hi".equalsIgnoreCase("hi")` → `true` |
| `.indexOf(s)` | int — first position | `"Hello".indexOf("l")` → `2` |
| `.split(regex)` | String[] — array | `"a,b,c".split(",")` → `["a","b","c"]` |

## Comparing Strings: equals() Not ==

Never use `==` to compare String content in Java:

```java
String a = "hello";
String b = "hello";
a == b          // might be true or false (compares memory addresses)
a.equals(b)     // always true — compares actual characters
```

Always use `.equals()` to compare string values.

In the code example below, you will see String methods applied to format and validate an email address.

codeExample.title: Email Formatter and Validator
codeExample.explanation:
- `.trim()` — removes all leading and trailing whitespace; does not affect characters in the middle
- `.toLowerCase()` — returns a new String with all characters in lowercase; strings are immutable in Java
- `.indexOf("@")` — returns the zero-based position of the first occurrence; returns `-1` if not found
- `.substring(0, atIndex)` — extracts characters from index `0` up to (not including) `atIndex`
- `.contains("@")` — returns `true` if the substring appears anywhere in the string
- `.replace("example.com", "codeguru.ai")` — returns a new string with all occurrences replaced

exercise.title: Build a Username Generator
exercise.instructions:
Write a Java program that generates a username from a full name. Given String fullName = "  Ananya Krishnan  ", generate a username by: (1) trimming whitespace, (2) converting to lowercase, (3) replacing the space between names with an underscore using replace(). Print the original trimmed name and the generated username on separate labelled lines.

Expected output:
Original: Ananya Krishnan
Username: ananya_krishnan
hints: 💡 Use lower.replace(" ", "_") to replace every space with an underscore and store it as username.
💡 .replace() returns a new String — assign it: String username = lower.replace(" ", "_");
💡 The trimmed name and lowercase steps are already done — username only needs the replace step applied to lower.

questions.question: Why must you use .equals() instead of == to compare two String values in Java?
options: == compares memory addresses of objects, not their character content
== only works with primitive types, so it throws an error for strings
.equals() is deprecated and should be replaced with ==
questions.explanation: In Java, == compares whether two references point to the exact same object in memory. Two String objects with identical content may be stored at different memory locations, causing == to return false even when the content matches. .equals() compares the actual character sequences, so it reliably returns true when the strings have the same content.

questions.question: What does "Hello".substring(1, 4) return in Java?
questions.explanation: substring(start, end) extracts characters from index start up to but not including index end. In "Hello", index 0 is 'H', index 1 is 'e', index 2 is 'l', index 3 is 'l'. substring(1, 4) extracts indices 1, 2, and 3, giving "ell". The character at index 4 ('o') is excluded because the end index is non-inclusive.
